title: Calorimetry: q = m·c·ΔT
blurb: Heat, mass, specific heat, temperature change — one equation ties them together: q = m·c·ΔT. These drills give you three and ask for the fourth, drawing each substance's specific heat from a sourced table (water 4.184, aluminum 0.897, and metals down to gold's 0.129 J/g·°C). Type your answer (rounded to three significant figures); if you slip, the drill names the mistake — most often treating every substance like water, or dropping a factor. The arithmetic is machine-checked; the specific heats are sourced and the calorimetry model is disclosed, not proved.
skills: solve q = m·c·ΔT for any single variable
read the right specific heat for the substance — each has its own
remember ΔT is a temperature difference (°C and K are interchangeable here)
keep the units straight: q in J, m in g, c in J/(g·°C), ΔT in °C

problems.prompt: Using q = m·c·ΔT: the specific heat of gold is c = 0.129 J/(g·°C), its temperature changes by 20 °C, and it absorbs 387 J of heat. Then what is the mass of the gold sample (g)?

diagnostics.misconception: Used the specific heat of another substance (4.184) instead of gold's c = 0.129 J/(g·°C).
Left out the specific heat — rearrange q = m·c·ΔT, keeping c = 0.129 J/(g·°C).

problems.explain: q = m·c·ΔT rearranges to m = q / (c·ΔT). Substituting the sourced c gives 150 g.

problems.prompt: Using q = m·c·ΔT: a 75 g sample of silicon, the specific heat of silicon is c = 0.712 J/(g·°C), and its temperature changes by 30 °C. Then how much heat does it absorb (J)?

diagnostics.misconception: Left out the specific heat — q = m·c·ΔT, not m·ΔT. Multiply by c = 0.712 J/(g·°C) too.
Used the specific heat of another substance (0.449) — silicon has its own, c = 0.712 J/(g·°C).

problems.explain: q = m·c·ΔT rearranges to q = m·c·ΔT. Substituting the sourced c gives 1600 J.

problems.prompt: Using q = m·c·ΔT: a 150 g sample of iron, the specific heat of iron is c = 0.449 J/(g·°C), and its temperature changes by 15 °C. Then how much heat does it absorb (J)?

diagnostics.misconception: Left out the specific heat — q = m·c·ΔT, not m·ΔT. Multiply by c = 0.449 J/(g·°C) too.
Used the specific heat of another substance (4.184) — iron has its own, c = 0.449 J/(g·°C).

problems.explain: q = m·c·ΔT rearranges to q = m·c·ΔT. Substituting the sourced c gives 1010 J.

problems.prompt: Using q = m·c·ΔT: a 150 g sample of copper, its temperature changes by 30 °C, and it absorbs 1733 J of heat. Then what is its specific heat (J/(g·°C))?

diagnostics.misconception: Divided by the mass only — c = q / (m·ΔT); you left out the temperature change.
Divided by the temperature change only — c = q / (m·ΔT); you left out the mass.

problems.explain: q = m·c·ΔT rearranges to c = q / (m·ΔT). Substituting the sourced c gives 0.385 J/(g·°C).

diagnostics.misconception: Divided by the mass only — c = q / (m·ΔT); you left out the temperature change.
Divided by the temperature change only — c = q / (m·ΔT); you left out the mass.

problems.explain: q = m·c·ΔT rearranges to c = q / (m·ΔT). Substituting the sourced c gives 0.897 J/(g·°C).

problems.prompt: Using q = m·c·ΔT: the specific heat of lead is c = 0.13 J/(g·°C), its temperature changes by 25 °C, and it absorbs 812.5 J of heat. Then what is the mass of the lead sample (g)?

diagnostics.misconception: Used the specific heat of another substance (0.897) instead of lead's c = 0.13 J/(g·°C).
Left out the specific heat — rearrange q = m·c·ΔT, keeping c = 0.13 J/(g·°C).

problems.explain: q = m·c·ΔT rearranges to m = q / (c·ΔT). Substituting the sourced c gives 250 g.

problems.prompt: Using q = m·c·ΔT: a 75 g sample of water, the specific heat of water is c = 4.184 J/(g·°C), and it absorbs 15690 J of heat. Then by how many degrees does it change (°C)?

diagnostics.misconception: Used the specific heat of another substance (0.129) instead of water's c = 4.184 J/(g·°C).
Left out the specific heat — rearrange q = m·c·ΔT, keeping c = 4.184 J/(g·°C).

problems.explain: q = m·c·ΔT rearranges to ΔT = q / (m·c). Substituting the sourced c gives 50 °C.

assumptions.claim: The calorimeter loses no heat to the surroundings — all the heat stays in the sample.
The specific heat capacity c is constant over the temperature range and there is no phase change (no melting or boiling).
